findings.specific_passage: She deserves to hear this before you've made the decision, not after. The fact that she left a stable job for this gives her the right to participate in knowing what's coming.
findings.challenge_statement: What concrete term, fallback, or enforcement step would still make the other side reciprocate if courtesy and good faith were taken out of the story?
findings.next_move: Map leverage, outside options, and enforcement first; only then decide whether the fairness norm is real or merely hoped for.
findings.intervention_hint: Map leverage, outside options, and enforcement first; only then decide whether the fairness norm is real or merely hoped for.

findings.specific_passage: If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.
findings.challenge_statement: What evidence is missing because the current story feels unusually salient or easy to recall?
findings.next_move: Step back, choose the best reference class you can defend, and compare the vivid case against that denominator before committing.
major_tensions: Availability correction is shallow when the team says 'be objective' but never changes the evidence denominator.
findings.intervention_hint: Step back, choose the best reference class you can defend, and compare the vivid case against that denominator before committing.

chunks.text: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
Is the worst case bad enough?
Confirmation Bias: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
chunks.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes the direct opposition: experimentation 'requires intellectual humility and willingness to seek disconfirming evidence' while confirmation bias drives processing information 'to reach a desired conclusion'.
chunks.activation_condition: When experiments are designed or interpreted to confirm the preferred hypothesis rather than genuinely testing whether it can be falsified

chunks.text: Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.

Select when: Uncertainty can be reduced faster and cheaper through targeted tests than through longer debate, forecasting, or opinion escalation. | Danger when: Experimentation has become theater, generating activity, dashboards, or post-hoc learning language without changing any real decision. | Reasoning: causal, diagnostic

anchors.evidence_quote: Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer proposes a targeted, low-cost pre-buy test to quickly reduce pivot uncertainty rather than extended debate or continuation, executing the experimentation mechanism.

chunks.text: Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.
Teams can use simplicity to hide uncertainty and present a rounded answer as if the underlying variance no longer matters.
Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.
Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
If this was cut, would anything be lost?

anchors.evidence_quote: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer insists on defining directional success thresholds upfront to avoid post-hoc rationalization and false precision in interpreting test results.

chunks.text: Alignment metrics can hide real divergence when dashboards or formal compliance are mistaken for actual goal alignment.
Diagnosis can go wrong when leaders confuse capability failure with capacity, system, or incentive failure.
If this outcome is bad, is the responsible party incapable and/or is the design bad?
Diagnose drift sequentially by separating goals, problems, root causes, design changes, and execution instead of blurring them together.
Define roles, decision boundaries, and expectations clearly enough that delegated work stays legible to the principal's actual objective.

anchors.evidence_quote: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.
anchors.presence_explanation: The answer explicitly distinguishes the operator's aligned decision authority from the passive shareholder's lack of operational say, executing principal-agent alignment.

That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias turns experimentation into hypothesis protection by designing or reading tests to support the favored story.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes the direct opposition: experimentation 'requires intellectual humility and willingness to seek disconfirming evidence' while confirmation bias drives processing information 'to reach a desired conclusion'.
expansions.activation_condition: When experiments are designed or interpreted to confirm the preferred hypothesis rather than genuinely testing whether it can be falsified

expansions.substrate_chunk: Experimentation conflicts with confirmation-bias when tests are designed or interpreted to confirm the hypothesis rather than genuinely challenge it.
expansions.why_relevant: Experimentation conflicts with confirmation-bias when tests are designed or interpreted to confirm the hypothesis rather than genuinely challenge it.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Narratives can resist experimental revision by making the expected story feel truer than the observed result.
expansions.why_relevant: Narratives can resist experimental revision by making the expected story feel truer than the observed result.
expansions.affinity_rationale: From tensions — 'compelling stories about expected outcomes resist revision even when experimental results contradict the narrative', creating emotional attachment to pre-experimental beliefs.
expansions.activation_condition: When a compelling narrative about expected outcomes creates emotional resistance to accepting experimental results that contradict the story

expansions.substrate_chunk: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
expansions.why_relevant: Anchoring makes false-precision-avoidance necessary because one precise number can dominate judgment long after it stops being the right guide.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical describes how 'people focus too much on a single, specific piece of information (the anchor), often a precise number, to misleadingly influence overall judgment' — directly opposes FPA's mission.
expansions.activation_condition: When a precise number anchors judgment and misleadingly influences the overall assessment, even though the number's precision exceeds the reliability of the underlying data

expansions.substrate_chunk: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.
expansions.why_relevant: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with base-rates when rounding away precision discards meaningful base-rate variation that would change the decision.

expansions.substrate_chunk: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with bayesian when avoidance of precise probability estimates prevents the quantitative prior-updating that Bayesian reasoning requires.
expansions.why_relevant: False-precision-avoidance conflicts with bayesian when avoidance of precise probability estimates prevents the quantitative prior-updating that Bayesian reasoning requires.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Confirmation-bias makes principal-agent-problem harder to diagnose because leaders can mistake mandate defense and dashboard compliance for genuine alignment.
expansions.why_relevant: Confirmation-bias makes principal-agent-problem harder to diagnose because leaders can mistake mandate defense and dashboard compliance for genuine alignment.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical risks: 'Premature Analysis/Confirmation Bias — the Principal rushes into analysis without being crystal clear about the output needed'.
expansions.activation_condition: When principals assume their initial hypothesis about agent performance is correct without rigorous testing.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Principal-agent-problem conflicts with information-asymmetry when the knowledge gap itself becomes the mechanism that hides divergence between principal goals and agent behavior.
expansions.why_relevant: Principal-agent-problem conflicts with information-asymmetry when the knowledge gap itself becomes the mechanism that hides divergence between principal goals and agent behavior.

expansions.substrate_chunk: Moral-hazard intensifies principal-agent-problem by making the agent's downside exposure too weak to keep behavior aligned with the principal's real risk profile.
expansions.why_relevant: Moral-hazard intensifies principal-agent-problem by making the agent's downside exposure too weak to keep behavior aligned with the principal's real risk profile.
expansions.affinity_rationale: Canonical: 'moral Hazard highlights the risk-taking behavior of agents when they do not bear the full consequences, intensifying the Principal-Agent Problem' — direct intensifier.
expansions.activation_condition: When agents take excessive risks because they do not bear the full consequences of their actions.

failure_hints.text: Conjunction-style optimism can make a complex experimental rollout look safer than it is because too many dependent conditions are assumed to hold.
Experimentation theater can produce dashboards and learning language without changing the underlying decision.
Teams can use simplicity to hide uncertainty and present a rounded answer as if the underlying variance no longer matters.
Simplification can fail by collapsing into vague slogans that no longer guide prediction, action, or alignment.
Alignment metrics can hide real divergence when dashboards or formal compliance are mistaken for actual goal alignment.
Diagnosis can go wrong when leaders confuse capability failure with capacity, system, or incentive failure.

heuristic_hints.text: Keep the experiment tied to a clear hypothesis and gather only enough facts to prove or disprove it.
Separate process steps like diagnosis and design so the test does not get blurred into premature solutioning.
Filter explanations by purpose so only detail that improves prediction, choice, or coordination survives.
Gather only enough facts to support or falsify the decision rather than boiling the ocean for decorative certainty.
Define roles, decision boundaries, and expectations clearly enough that delegated work stays legible to the principal's actual objective.
Diagnose drift sequentially by separating goals, problems, root causes, design changes, and execution instead of blurring them together.

premortem_hints.text: Is the worst case bad enough?
What do you think the other side doesn't get about your perspective?
If this was cut, would anything be lost?
If this outcome is bad, is the responsible party incapable and/or is the design bad?

select_when: Uncertainty can be reduced faster and cheaper through targeted tests than through longer debate, forecasting, or opinion escalation.
Each experiment can be tied to a clear hypothesis, success threshold, and downstream decision instead of being run for its own sake.
Reversible probes can inform larger irreversible commitments, reallocations, or product choices.
The team is stuck in argument about what is true, but a structured test could resolve the dispute.
danger_when: Experimentation has become theater, generating activity, dashboards, or post-hoc learning language without changing any real decision.
Local test wins are being overgeneralized while the team ignores system interactions, long-term effects, or behavior changes outside the test window.

select_when: A decision needs directional clarity, bounded ranges, or operating thresholds more than an illusion of exact certainty.
Extra decimal places, caveats, or scenario branches are consuming attention without changing the decision that would actually be made.
The audience needs to act, align, or prioritize quickly and will be harmed more by cluttered precision than by a well-calibrated approximation.
The current explanation is accurate enough to sound rigorous but too overloaded to be useful for prediction, choice, or coordination.
danger_when: Simplicity is being used to hide uncertainty, skip the underlying analysis, or present a rounded answer as if the variance no longer matters.
The model is simplifying so far that the result becomes vague, slogan-like, or detached from the assumptions that make it usable.

select_when: Work is being delegated across an objective gap where the executing party may benefit from choices that do not serve the owner of the outcome.
Specialized knowledge, hidden intentions, or role ambiguity make it hard to tell whether the agent's behavior is aligned with the principal's real goals.
The main challenge is to design incentives, oversight, and role clarity so delegated work stays tied to the larger objective.
Dashboards or formal compliance are starting to look like substitutes for actual alignment, so the team needs to inspect divergence underneath the metrics.
danger_when: The response is collapsing into micromanagement of the current case instead of diagnosing the machine-level design that created the drift.
The model is being used to assume bad faith when the main issue is capability, capacity, or a muddled objective rather than a true alignment split.

frame_elements.element_text: pivot now while we have runway and a real signal, or push harder on the current product
frame_elements.evidence_quote: I'm trying to decide: pivot now while we have runway and a real signal, or push harder on the current product.
frame_elements.fragility_signal: existence of other strategic options like gradual transition, feature hybridization, or acquisition/sale of the product

frame_elements.element_text: three customers tell us unprompted that they'd pay way more for a specific workflow tool
frame_elements.evidence_quote: we've had three customers tell us unprompted that they'd pay way more for a specific workflow tool we could build
frame_elements.fragility_signal: signal is merely conversational interest without commitments, prices, or timelines

reframings.reframed_question: What if we gradually integrate the workflow tool as a premium feature into the current product before fully pivoting?
reframings.what_opens: Explores hybrid strategies that preserve existing 22 customers and $4K MRR while testing the new workflow signal without full commitment.

reframings.reframed_question: How strong is the signal from the three customers if we ask them for specific pricing commitments and timelines to build the workflow tool?
reframings.what_opens: Tests the fragility of conversational interest by seeking concrete commitments, distinguishing enthusiasm from validated demand.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Distinguishes sunk costs implicitly by focusing on future runway and test before pivot; identifies exit costs for employees (layoff/severance), customers (transition plans), and co-founder (communication to preserve relationship); recommends staging via pre-buy test to preserve optionality rather than full commitment now.

No modeling of competitors' responses, retaliation, imitation, preemption, or first-mover dynamics in dental SaaS space; mentions selling customer list to competitor but doesn't analyze competitive reactions to pivot.
dimensions.materiality_note: Competitors could respond to pivot by filling gaps in current or new product, potentially altering pivot viability or timing.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Identifies employee incentives (CS role pivots to busywork vs layoff), co-founder as passive shareholder (inform but no say to align with operator role); discusses monitoring via honesty and pre-communication to prevent resentment; addresses divergence if uninformed.

Acknowledges can't keep both employees on pivot (engineer stays, CS goes) but doesn't compare marginal returns of keeping CS for current product push vs pivot, or what runway/time is given up by maintaining current customers/product.
dimensions.materiality_note: With 14 months runway and 2 employees, misallocating scarce engineering time or burn rate could deplete runway faster, changing no-pivot viability.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Names risks (test fails, employee layoff, customer unhappiness) but doesn't size downside exposure (e.g., probability/impact of pivot failure draining runway), distinguish mitigate vs adapt strategies, or detail recovery paths beyond transition plans.
dimensions.materiality_note: Pivot risks full runway loss vs current product's flat-but-alive status; sizing tail risks like total failure could favor no-pivot.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Identifies stakeholders (22 customers: transition plans; CS employee: layoff/pivot options with honesty; co-founder: inform without veto); provides influence strategy (early honesty, severance, references) distinguishing technical pivot from organizational feasibility.

Specifies sequence (pre-buy test first, then employee/co-founder talks, decision in 14 days); rationale for order (test before commitments to avoid premature lock-in); addresses delay costs (runway shrink, uncertainty drags).

Classifies conversational signal as weak (epistemic uncertainty reducible via pre-buy test) vs decision-grade, but doesn't distinguish aleatory risks (e.g., market adoption randomness) or calibrate overall confidence to evidence; treats test as resolving all uncertainty.
dimensions.materiality_note: Signal from 3 customers is noisy; recognizing higher Knightian uncertainty in new workflow demand could favor current product stability.

dimensions.coverage_evidence: Assesses signal quality (unprompted but conversational vs commercial; existing vs prospect; needs pre-buy to upgrade); distinguishes noise ('like the idea') from signal ('will pay'); tests via specific experiment.

questions: What competitors in dental SaaS are already building or offering similar workflow tools to what your three customers mentioned?
If you pivot to the workflow tool, which existing dental practice software providers might respond by adding that feature to retain customers like your 22 paying ones?
Who in the dental space could partner with you on the new workflow tool instead of competing, and what would they gain from it?
What specific skills or time from your engineer could be redirected from the current product to the workflow tool pivot without delaying critical bug fixes for the 22 customers?
Beyond the engineer and CS role, what other resources like your own time or budget could be reallocated to test demand for the new product faster?
If runway shrinks below 14 months during the pivot, which of the 22 customers or other assets could you monetize or cut to extend it?
What would be the worst-case fallout for your 22 paying customers if you pivot aggressively without their full buy-in?
How much more runway buffer would you need to feel comfortable adapting to a pivot rather than mitigating risks by sticking with the current flat product?
If the pre-buy test fails, what aggressive steps could you take with the current product to adapt rather than just pushing harder?
For the conversational signals from your three customers, what hard data like usage patterns in your current product could measure the risk of their interest?
Beyond the pre-buy test, what unknown factors about dental practices' workflow needs create true uncertainty that no data could resolve soon?
What aspects of managing your two employees or former co-founder's reaction during a pivot feel like complete ignorance rather than calculable risks?

triage_scores.evidence: Faint signal in Turn 5 where assistant recommends informing passive ex-co-founder 'as a friend' to preserve relationship, but does not distort facts, ignore faults, or let affection override the clear advice that she has no decision say.

Assistant pushes for a 14-day decision deadline and pre-committing to a bar before tests (Turn 6), closing uncertainty prematurely despite 14-month runway and weak conversational signals, omitting extended validation options.
Faint signal in acknowledging employee loyalty and customer retention concerns (Turns 3-4), but reasoning tests these rather than superreacting to losses.

In Turn 4, assistant commissions 'The fact that she left a stable job for this gives her the right to participate in knowing what's coming' and in Turn 5 'Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem,' explicitly invoking reciprocal fairness norms as a driver for informing the employee and ex-co-founder, materially shaping recommendations without testing against pure business incentives.

Assistant initially overweighted the vivid 'three customers unprompted' signal as 'stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR' without referencing the 22 existing customers or $4K MRR base rate, only later qualifying it after user clarification, treating the small vivid sample as decision-driving relative to the broader denominator.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Distinguish between conversational interest and commercial commitment to properly assess signal strength
evidence_quotes: "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR.
The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial.
Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes establish the distinction between types of signals and conclude that the current signal lacks decision-grade strength.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Propose a pre-buy test with specific criteria to validate the pivot signal quickly
evidence_quotes: Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI.

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?"
If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes define the test, its execution, and pass criteria to convert conversational signal into actionable commitment.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Recommend structured transition options for existing customers to avoid pivot drag
evidence_quotes: Don't kill it — transition it. If you pivot, the 22 customers need a landing zone. Options: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate;
The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes outline transition strategies and warn against common pitfalls that hinder pivots.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Evaluate employee management options prioritizing honesty and clean outcomes
evidence_quotes: You have three options and none of them are painless. First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can), and a genuine reference and support in finding her next role.
Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected. I want you to know so you can think about what you want to do."
My honest read: option three, then option one if the pivot confirms.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes present, compare, and recommend sequenced options for handling the CS employee.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Advise informing passive equity holder transparently without granting decision input
evidence_quotes: She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.
Tell her before you run the pre-buy test with the three customers. Frame it as: "I'm considering a pivot. Here's the signal. Here's what I'm going to test. I wanted you to know before I do it."
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes specify communication protocol balancing courtesy with authority retention.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Insist on setting a firm decision deadline and predefined success criteria to prevent delay
evidence_quotes: Set a decision date. Right now your list ends with "commit or walk" but doesn't have a when. Without a when, the list becomes three more months of "considering the pivot"
Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call.

before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes mandate timeline and criteria to enforce decisive action.

companion_fingerprint_raw.reasoning_move: Identify hard conversations as the core bottleneck unlocking the pivot decision
evidence_quotes: The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working.
Have the conversations. The strategic answer will fall out of them.
companion_fingerprint_raw.evidence_rationale: These quotes diagnose root cause and prescribe action to resolve it.

companion_fingerprint_validated.reasoning_move: Distinguish between conversational interest and commercial commitment to properly assess signal strength
evidence_quotes: "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR.
The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial.
Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade.
companion_fingerprint_validated.evidence_rationale: These quotes establish the distinction between types of signals and conclude that the current signal lacks decision-grade strength.

That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

query:
Solo founder deciding whether to pivot B2B SaaS product from current offering to new workflow tool amid flat growth, $4K MRR, 14 months runway, and 22 customers.

Constraints stated during conversation:
- [ACTIVE] Runway 14 months at current burn
- [ACTIVE] 22 paying customers on current product
- [ACTIVE] Two full-time employees (engineer, CS)
- [ACTIVE] Conversational signal from three customers, no commitments
- [ACTIVE] Former co-founder holds 15% equity, passive

Original framing: Founder assumes pivot signal from three customers is valid; weighs pivoting now with 14 months runway vs pushing current flat product; excludes other options beyond these two.
vanilla_answer:
SYNTHESIZED POSITION:
Run pre-buy test this week with the three customers at specific price ($X, 3 months prepay); define pass bar upfront (e.g., 2/3 commit); set decision in 14 days; if pass, pivot with transition plan for current customers (freeze/sunset/migrate), inform/talk to CS employee about uncertainty then likely layoff with severance, inform former co-founder without seeking input; have hard conversations first as they unlock the decision.

FULL ASSISTANT REASONING:
A few things worth separating. "Three customers unprompted" is a real signal, not a weak one. Unprompted signal at $4K MRR is stronger than prompted signal at $50K MRR. But I want to ask: are those three customers in your existing customer base, or are they prospects you've been pitching? And has the conversation gone past "we'd pay more for that" into "here's what I'd pay, here's when, here's what it would replace"?

The difference matters because "we'd pay more for that" is conversational; "I would switch from ToolX and pay $400/month for this by Q3" is commercial. Those two signals look the same on a gut level but they're different enough to drive the decision.

---

OK — that changes the weight. Conversational signal is real but not decision-grade. Before you pivot, you need two or three customers to commit to pre-buying the new product at a specific price point, ideally with a deposit or at minimum a signed LOI. If they won't do that, the signal you have is "people like the idea of the thing" which is qualitatively different from "people will pay for the thing."

Here's the test I'd run this week: go back to the three and say "we're considering building this. If we commit to building it in the next 90 days, would you be willing to pre-pay three months of the new product at $X?" Pick a price. See what they say. That conversation will tell you more than three more months of the current product would.

If two of three say yes, you have enough signal to pivot. If all three pass when money is actually involved, you have a feature idea, not a product.

---

Don't kill it — transition it. If you pivot, the 22 customers need a landing zone. Options: freeze the product (stop new features, keep it running, offer to migrate them to the new product when ready); or sunset it with 6-month notice and a discount on the new product for those who migrate; or sell the customer list to a competitor, which is rare at your scale but not impossible in vertical SaaS.

The worst version is treating the current customers as an obligation that drags the pivot. You'll keep doing bug fixes and small features "just for them" and the pivot will take 50% longer than it should. Be honest with them early about what's happening and give them a clean option.

On your fear — it's real, and it's also the thing that often kills pivots. Founders delay the hard customer conversation because they're protecting their own hope. The customers know something is wrong when growth is flat for four months. Your honesty won't surprise them; it will relieve them.

---

You have three options and none of them are painless.

First: lay her off cleanly, with severance appropriate to your ability to pay (2-4 weeks minimum, more if you can), and a genuine reference and support in finding her next role. This is the most common answer and often the best one. You don't drag her through a project where she has nothing to do.

Second: pivot her role. Is there something she could do during the build period — market research, early pipeline development for the new product, partnership conversations? If the work is real and useful, this can work. If you're inventing busywork, she'll see through it immediately and it's worse than the layoff.

Third: be honest with her now about the uncertainty. "I'm considering a pivot. If I do it, your role is likely affected. I want you to know so you can think about what you want to do. If you decide to look around, I'll support that and I'll still give you severance when the pivot happens." Some people appreciate the advance notice; others will leave immediately, which may or may not suit you.

My honest read: option three, then option one if the pivot confirms. She deserves to hear this before you've made the decision, not after. The fact that she left a stable job for this gives her the right to participate in knowing what's coming.

---

She doesn't get a say in the decision — you're the operator, she's a passive shareholder at this point. But she deserves to be informed, and not after the fact.

Tell her before you run the pre-buy test with the three customers. Frame it as: "I'm considering a pivot. Here's the signal. Here's what I'm going to test. I wanted you to know before I do it." You're not asking for permission; you're keeping her in the loop as someone who still owns part of the company.

This matters less for the pivot decision itself and more for your relationship with her. Passive shareholders who find out about major strategic decisions via email announcements get resentful, and friend-shareholders who feel cut out become ex-friends. Five minutes of courtesy now prevents a multi-year relationship problem.

---

One thing. Set a decision date. Right now your list ends with "commit or walk" but doesn't have a when. Without a when, the list becomes three more months of "considering the pivot" while your runway shrinks and your employees wonder what's happening.

Give yourself 14 days. After the pre-buy test and the three conversations, you make the call. Not "we'll see how it feels" — an actual date on your calendar where you decide. The decision is easier when you've pre-committed to making it.

Also worth adding: before you run the pre-buy test, write down what "pass" looks like. Two of three commit? All three? What dollar amount? If you decide the bar after seeing the results, you'll negotiate with yourself into whatever answer you want. Bar first, data second.

---

Of course. One last thing. The thing that ate the month wasn't indecision about the pivot — it was avoidance of the hard conversations: with the customers who might say no, with the employee who might be laid off, with the friend who might feel excluded, with yourself about a product that isn't working. Those conversations are the bottleneck, not the strategic question.

Have the conversations. The strategic answer will fall out of them. Good luck.
